sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigating hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC, also known as Lynch syndrome) studied the role of mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency in tumor development. They obtained tissue biopsies from 40 patients diagnosed with HNPCC and 40 age-matched controls with sporadic colorectal tumors. Genomic DNA was extracted and subjected to restriction enzyme digestion with EcoRI, which recognizes the palindromic sequence 5′-GAATTC-3′. The digested DNA was separated by agarose gel electrophoresis and visualized with ethidium bromide staining (Figure 1).

Microsatellite instability (MSI) analysis was performed by amplifying five standard microsatellite markers (BAT-25, BAT-26, D2S123, D5S346, D17S250). Tumors showing instability at two or more markers were classified as MSI-high (MSI-H). Among HNPCC patients, 85% were MSI-H, compared to 15% of sporadic cases. Immunohistochemistry revealed loss of MLH1 protein expression in 60% of HNPCC tumors and loss of MSH2 in 30%. The remaining 10% showed intact MMR protein expression but carried missense mutations in MSH6 identified by Sanger sequencing.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Restriction digest gel electrophoresis of genomic DNA. Lane 1: DNA ladder. Lane 2: wild-type (WT) control. Lanes 3–5: Patient mutant samples (Mut A, Mut B, Mut C).
questions.stem: The microsatellite instability observed in HNPCC tumors is most directly caused by:

choices.A: increased activity of DNA polymerase δ at repetitive sequences, leading to frameshift insertions.
choices.B: defective nucleotide excision repair (NER), which normally removes bulky adducts from repetitive DNA.
choices.C: failure of the mismatch repair system to correct replication slippage errors at short tandem repeat sequences.
choices.D: loss of proofreading function in DNA polymerase ε, causing base substitutions throughout the genome.

explanations.A: DNA polymerase δ does not have increased activity in HNPCC. Rather, the polymerase makes replication errors at its normal rate, but the MMR system that would ordinarily correct insertion/deletion loops (IDLs) at microsatellites is non-functional. The problem is failed repair, not enhanced error generation.
explanations.B: Nucleotide excision repair (NER) removes bulky, helix-distorting lesions such as thymine dimers and chemical adducts. It does not act on the small insertion/deletion loops that form during replication slippage at microsatellites. HNPCC is caused by defects in mismatch repair (MMR) genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6), not NER genes (XPA, XPB, XPD).
explanations.C: Correct. Microsatellites are short tandem repeat sequences (1–6 bp units repeated multiple times) where DNA polymerase is prone to replication slippage—the template and newly synthesized strands transiently denature and re-anneal out of register, creating insertion/deletion loops (IDLs). Normally, the MMR system (MutSα/MutLα complexes involving MSH2/MSH6 and MLH1/PMS2) recognizes these IDLs and excises the error-containing strand. In HNPCC, loss of MMR function allows these slippage errors to persist, causing microsatellite length changes across cell divisions—the hallmark of MSI.
explanations.D: DNA polymerase ε proofreading deficiency (POLE mutations) causes an ultramutated phenotype with predominantly base substitutions, not the insertion/deletion pattern characteristic of microsatellite instability. POLE-mutant tumors are MSI-stable and show a distinct mutational signature from MMR-deficient tumors.

questions.stem: EcoRI recognizes the sequence 5′-GAATTC-3′. What type of ends does this enzyme generate, and why?

choices.A: Blunt ends, because EcoRI cuts both strands at the center of the recognition site simultaneously.
choices.B: Sticky ends with 5′ overhangs, because EcoRI makes staggered cuts between the G and A on each strand of the palindromic sequence.
choices.C: Sticky ends with 3′ overhangs, because EcoRI cleaves after the terminal C on each strand.
choices.D: Blunt ends with phosphorylated 3′ termini, because type II restriction enzymes always produce blunt cuts.

explanations.A: Blunt ends result from cuts directly across from each other on both strands (e.g., SmaI cutting at CCC|GGG). EcoRI does not cut at the center; it cleaves between G and A on each strand (G|AATTC and CTTAA|G), producing staggered cuts offset by four nucleotides.
explanations.B: Correct. EcoRI is a type II restriction endonuclease that cleaves the palindromic sequence 5′-G|AATTC-3′ / 3′-CTTAA|G-5′. The cut occurs between the G and the first A on each strand. Because the cuts are staggered (not opposite each other), the result is 4-nucleotide 5′ single-stranded overhangs (5′-AATT-3′). These cohesive (“sticky”) ends can base-pair with complementary overhangs from other EcoRI-digested fragments, facilitating cloning and ligation.
explanations.C: 3′ overhangs are produced by enzymes like KpnI (GGTAC|C) that cut closer to the 3′ end of the recognition sequence. EcoRI’s staggered cut between G and A on each strand produces 5′ overhangs, not 3′ overhangs. The distinction matters for downstream cloning applications.
explanations.D: Not all type II restriction enzymes produce blunt ends. Type II enzymes vary in their cut patterns: some produce blunt ends (e.g., SmaI, HaeIII), some 5′ overhangs (e.g., EcoRI, BamHI), and some 3′ overhangs (e.g., KpnI, PstI). The cut pattern depends on the enzyme’s specific interaction with its recognition sequence.

questions.stem: In the gel electrophoresis image, Mut A shows a single band at approximately 1,050 bp instead of the two bands seen in the wild-type lane (300 bp and 750 bp). Which of the following best explains this observation?

choices.A: A point mutation in Mut A has eliminated one EcoRI recognition site, preventing cleavage at that position and producing a single larger fragment.
choices.B: Mut A has a deletion of 700 bp that removes one of the two fragments entirely.
choices.C: The Mut A sample was not fully digested, so the intact genomic fragment migrated as a single band.
choices.D: Mut A has a chromosomal translocation that moved one fragment to a different chromosome.

explanations.A: Correct. The wild-type shows two bands at 300 bp and 750 bp, summing to 1,050 bp. Mut A shows a single band at approximately 1,050 bp, matching this sum exactly. This pattern is consistent with a point mutation that changed one of the six nucleotides in the internal EcoRI site (5′-GAATTC-3′), abolishing enzyme recognition. Without cleavage at that site, the entire 1,050 bp region migrates as one fragment. Even a single base change in the palindrome prevents EcoRI binding and cutting.
explanations.B: If Mut A had a 700 bp deletion, the total DNA in that region would be approximately 350 bp (1,050 − 700), not 1,050 bp. The band would migrate much faster on the gel, appearing below the 500 bp ladder marker. The observed band at 1,050 bp rules out a large deletion.
explanations.C: Incomplete digestion would typically show the 1,050 bp uncut band plus partial digestion products (the 300 and 750 bp fragments). The absence of any smaller bands in the Mut A lane, combined with a clean single band, is more consistent with a genuine loss of the EcoRI site than with partial digestion. Controls would confirm complete digestion of other sites.
explanations.D: A translocation would move a fragment to a different chromosomal location but would not eliminate the EcoRI site itself. The restriction fragment containing the recognition site would still be cut and would appear on the gel, though possibly at a different size if the translocation breakpoint fell within the fragment.

questions.stem: The passage states that 10% of HNPCC tumors showed intact MMR protein expression but carried missense mutations in MSH6. These tumors are most likely MSI-positive because:

choices.A: MSH6 is not involved in mismatch repair and its mutation causes genomic instability through an independent pathway.
choices.B: missense mutations always cause complete loss of protein function, identical to a null mutation.
choices.C: MSH6 mutations prevent DNA polymerase from binding to the replication fork, increasing error rates.
choices.D: the missense mutation likely disrupts MSH6 protein function (e.g., mismatch recognition or ATPase activity) while preserving antigenicity detected by immunohistochemistry.

explanations.A: MSH6 is a core component of the MMR system. It forms the MutSα heterodimer with MSH2, which recognizes single base mismatches and small insertion/deletion loops. Mutations in MSH6 directly impair mismatch recognition, causing the MSI phenotype through the canonical MMR pathway, not an alternative mechanism.
explanations.B: Missense mutations result in amino acid substitutions that can have variable effects ranging from no functional impact to complete loss of function, depending on the residue affected. Unlike nonsense or frameshift mutations, missense variants may produce stable, folded proteins that retain some epitopes recognized by antibodies but lose catalytic or binding function.
explanations.C: MSH6 does not interact with the replication fork or DNA polymerase. Its role is post-replicative: the MutSα complex (MSH2/MSH6) slides along newly replicated DNA scanning for mismatches. Mutations in MSH6 impair mismatch detection, not polymerase loading or processivity.
explanations.D: Correct. Immunohistochemistry detects protein presence using antibodies against specific epitopes, but it does not assess functional activity. A missense mutation can change a critical residue in MSH6’s mismatch-binding domain or ATPase domain, abolishing repair function while preserving the protein’s overall folded structure and the epitopes recognized by the diagnostic antibody. This explains how a tumor can stain positive for MSH6 on IHC yet exhibit MSI due to a non-functional protein.

questions.stem: During normal DNA replication, the mismatch repair system must distinguish the newly synthesized strand from the template strand. In E. coli, this is accomplished by:

choices.A: the presence of 5-methylcytosine on the template strand, which is absent from the new strand until a maintenance methyltransferase acts.
choices.B: dam methylase-mediated adenine methylation at GATC sites, which is present on the parental strand but transiently absent from the new strand.
choices.C: histone acetylation marks on the template strand that are not yet deposited on newly replicated chromatin.
choices.D: the RNA primer remnants on the lagging strand, which signal the repair machinery to excise errors from that strand.

explanations.A: 5-methylcytosine is a modification found primarily in eukaryotic DNA (at CpG dinucleotides) and plays a role in gene regulation and epigenetics, not in E. coli mismatch repair. E. coli uses N⁶-methyladenine (not 5-methylcytosine) at GATC sequences for strand discrimination.
explanations.B: Correct. In E. coli, the dam methyltransferase methylates adenine at the N⁶ position within 5′-GATC-3′ sequences. Immediately after replication, only the parental (template) strand is methylated; the new strand is transiently unmethylated until dam methylase acts (a brief hemimethylated state). The MutH endonuclease specifically recognizes hemimethylated GATC sites and nicks the unmethylated (new) strand, directing the MMR system to excise and resynthesize that strand. This ensures errors are removed from the daughter strand, not the template.
explanations.C: E. coli is a prokaryote and does not package its DNA with histones. Histone modifications are a eukaryotic chromatin feature. While eukaryotic MMR does use mechanisms distinct from dam methylation for strand discrimination (possibly involving strand breaks and PCNA orientation), histone acetylation is not the primary signal.
explanations.D: RNA primers are removed during Okazaki fragment maturation by RNase H and DNA polymerase I before ligation by DNA ligase. While strand breaks may play a role in eukaryotic MMR strand discrimination, in E. coli the primary mechanism is dam methylation at GATC sites, not RNA primer remnants.

questions.passage:
A separate study examined telomerase activity across different cell types to understand replicative aging and its implications for cancer biology. The researchers isolated protein extracts from four cell populations: embryonic stem cells (ESCs), adult hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs), differentiated fibroblasts, and HeLa cancer cells. Telomerase activity was measured using the telomeric repeat amplification protocol (TRAP) assay, which detects the enzyme’s ability to extend a synthetic telomere substrate in vitro.

Results showed high TRAP activity in ESCs and HeLa cells, moderate activity in HSCs, and undetectable activity in fibroblasts. Mean telomere length was measured by terminal restriction fragment (TRF) analysis: ESCs = 12.5 kb, HSCs = 8.2 kb, fibroblasts = 5.1 kb, and HeLa cells = 4.8 kb. Population doubling experiments revealed that fibroblasts ceased dividing after approximately 55 passages (the Hayflick limit), while ESCs and HeLa cells showed unlimited proliferative capacity. The researchers also measured expression of TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase) and TERC (telomerase RNA component) by RT-qPCR.
questions.stem: HeLa cells maintain unlimited proliferative capacity despite having shorter telomeres than fibroblasts at the Hayflick limit. This observation is best explained by:

choices.A: constitutive telomerase activity in HeLa cells that maintains telomere length above the critical threshold for senescence, even though their equilibrium length is shorter than that of early-passage fibroblasts.
choices.B: HeLa cells having mutated shelterin proteins that prevent telomere shortening regardless of telomerase activity.
choices.C: HeLa cells using the alternative lengthening of telomeres (ALT) pathway exclusively, which does not require a minimum telomere length.
choices.D: the TRAP assay producing a false positive for HeLa cells due to non-specific primer amplification.

explanations.A: Correct. The key distinction is not absolute telomere length but whether telomeres are maintained above the critical length that triggers cellular senescence via the DNA damage response. Fibroblasts lack telomerase, so their telomeres shorten progressively with each division (losing ~50–200 bp per division). At ~5.1 kb average, some telomeres have likely reached the critical threshold, activating p53/Rb-mediated senescence. HeLa cells express constitutive telomerase, which continuously adds TTAGGG repeats, maintaining telomeres at a stable (though short) equilibrium length of ~4.8 kb. This equilibrium represents a balance between shortening during replication and telomerase-mediated extension, keeping all telomeres above the senescence threshold.
explanations.B: While shelterin complex proteins (TRF1, TRF2, POT1, etc.) are essential for telomere protection, mutations in these proteins typically cause telomere dysfunction and genome instability rather than preventing shortening. HeLa cells maintain telomeres through active telomerase, as confirmed by the high TRAP assay signal, not through passive protection.
explanations.C: The TRAP assay directly measures telomerase catalytic activity, and HeLa cells showed high TRAP activity, confirming active telomerase. While ALT is used by some cancer cells (typically telomerase-negative), the positive TRAP result indicates HeLa cells rely primarily on telomerase, not ALT. ALT-dependent cells typically show heterogeneous telomere lengths, not the relatively uniform 4.8 kb observed.
explanations.D: The TRAP assay includes multiple controls to rule out non-specific amplification, including heat-inactivated enzyme controls and RNase pretreatment. HeLa cells are one of the most well-characterized telomerase-positive cell lines in biology, and their high TRAP activity has been independently confirmed by numerous laboratories.

questions.stem: Telomerase is classified as a reverse transcriptase because it:

choices.A: converts mRNA into cDNA for insertion at chromosome ends.
choices.B: replicates telomeric DNA using a DNA template carried within the enzyme complex.
choices.C: synthesizes DNA using its integral RNA component (TERC) as the template for adding telomeric repeats.
choices.D: transcribes telomeric DNA into RNA, which is then reverse-transcribed by a separate polymerase.

explanations.A: Telomerase does not act on mRNA or produce cDNA. It specifically extends the 3′ G-rich overhang of telomeric DNA by adding hexameric TTAGGG repeats. The template is an internal RNA component (TERC), not cellular mRNA. Retroviral reverse transcriptases convert mRNA to cDNA, but telomerase serves a distinct biological function.
explanations.B: Telomerase does not carry a DNA template. Its catalytic subunit TERT uses the RNA subunit TERC as a template. TERC contains a short sequence complementary to the telomeric repeat (3′-CAAUCCCAAUC-5′ in humans) that directs synthesis of the TTAGGG DNA repeat. The RNA-to-DNA templating defines it as a reverse transcriptase.
explanations.C: Correct. Telomerase is a ribonucleoprotein complex with two essential components: TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase), the catalytic protein subunit, and TERC (telomerase RNA component), which contains an 11-nucleotide template sequence complementary to the telomeric repeat. TERT uses the RNA template in TERC to synthesize telomeric DNA (TTAGGG repeats) at chromosome 3′ ends. This RNA→DNA templating activity defines telomerase as a reverse transcriptase, distinguishing it from conventional DNA-dependent DNA polymerases.
explanations.D: Telomerase does not transcribe DNA into RNA. It performs the reverse: synthesizing DNA from an RNA template. The TERC component is constitutively present as part of the holoenzyme complex and is not produced by transcribing telomeric DNA during each extension cycle. TERC is transcribed from its own gene by RNA polymerase II.

questions.stem: The fibroblasts in the study reached the Hayflick limit at approximately 55 passages. Which molecular pathway is most directly responsible for triggering this replicative senescence?

choices.A: Accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that directly inhibit cyclin-dependent kinases.
choices.B: Epigenetic silencing of growth factor receptors through promoter hypermethylation.
choices.C: Depletion of nucleotide pools due to extensive DNA replication over many divisions.
choices.D: Critically short telomeres that are recognized as DNA double-strand breaks, activating the p53/p21 and Rb/p16 tumor suppressor pathways.

explanations.A: While ROS can contribute to cellular aging through oxidative damage, they are not the primary trigger for replicative senescence at the Hayflick limit. The defining feature of replicative senescence is its direct link to telomere shortening, not generalized oxidative stress. ROS-induced senescence (stress-induced premature senescence) operates through a distinct mechanism.
explanations.B: While epigenetic changes occur during aging, promoter hypermethylation of growth factor receptors is not the primary mechanism driving the Hayflick limit. Replicative senescence is specifically triggered by telomere attrition, which activates DNA damage signaling independent of growth factor receptor status.
explanations.C: Cells maintain adequate nucleotide pools through de novo synthesis and salvage pathways even after extensive replication. Nucleotide depletion would cause replication fork stalling and S-phase arrest, not the G1 arrest characteristic of replicative senescence. The Hayflick limit is telomere-dependent, not nucleotide-dependent.
explanations.D: Correct. Each cell division shortens telomeres by 50–200 bp because DNA polymerase cannot fully replicate the 3′ end of linear chromosomes (the end-replication problem). After ~55 divisions, some telomeres reach a critically short length where the protective shelterin complex can no longer form a proper T-loop structure. The exposed chromosome end is recognized by ATM/ATR kinases as a DNA double-strand break, triggering a persistent DNA damage response (DDR). This activates p53, which upregulates p21 (a CDK inhibitor) to arrest cells in G1. Simultaneously, p16ᶥ᷺ᵏᵃ⁴ᵃ accumulates and inhibits CDK4/6, reinforcing Rb-mediated cell cycle arrest.

questions.stem: The end-replication problem specifically affects which aspect of DNA replication?

choices.A: Removal of the terminal RNA primer on the lagging strand leaves a gap that cannot be filled by DNA polymerase, resulting in progressive 5′-end shortening.
choices.B: DNA polymerase ε dissociates from the leading strand template before reaching the chromosome terminus.
choices.C: Topoisomerase II cannot resolve the final supercoils at the replication fork termination site.
choices.D: DNA ligase cannot seal the nick between the last two Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand.

explanations.A: Correct. During lagging strand synthesis, RNA primers are required to initiate each Okazaki fragment. After replication, most primers are removed by RNase H/FEN1 and the gaps are filled by DNA polymerase. However, the terminal RNA primer at the very 5′ end of the lagging strand daughter molecule cannot be replaced with DNA because there is no upstream 3′-OH to serve as a priming site for DNA polymerase. When this primer is removed, a short single-stranded gap remains, and the daughter strand is shorter than the parent. Over successive rounds of replication, this causes progressive telomere shortening.
explanations.B: DNA polymerase ε (or δ, depending on the model) is highly processive when associated with the PCNA sliding clamp and can replicate to the end of a linear template. The leading strand is synthesized continuously and does not inherently lose terminal sequences; the end-replication problem is specific to the lagging strand.
explanations.C: Topoisomerase II resolves catenanes (interlinked daughter chromosomes) after replication, which is a separate process from the end-replication problem. Topoisomerase activity at the terminus does not cause sequence loss; the shortening is due to primer removal on the lagging strand.
explanations.D: DNA ligase seals nicks between Okazaki fragments after gap-filling by DNA polymerase. Ligase function is not impaired at chromosome ends. The problem is not nick sealing but rather the inability to fill the gap left by the terminal RNA primer after its removal.

questions.stem: If the researchers treated HeLa cell extracts with RNase before performing the TRAP assay, the expected result would be:

choices.A: unchanged telomerase activity, because TERT alone can synthesize telomeric DNA without an RNA template.
choices.B: abolished telomerase activity, because destruction of TERC eliminates the template required for reverse transcription of telomeric repeats.
choices.C: increased telomerase activity, because RNase removes inhibitory RNA molecules that normally suppress TERT catalysis.
choices.D: partial reduction in activity, because RNase degrades only cytoplasmic TERC and cannot access the nuclear pool.

explanations.A: TERT cannot function without its RNA template component. Telomerase is an obligate ribonucleoprotein; the catalytic activity of TERT depends on TERC providing the template sequence for reverse transcription. Without TERC, TERT has no information specifying what DNA sequence to synthesize and cannot add telomeric repeats.
explanations.B: Correct. Telomerase requires both TERT (the reverse transcriptase) and TERC (the RNA template) for catalytic activity. RNase treatment degrades TERC, eliminating the template sequence that TERT uses to direct synthesis of TTAGGG repeats. Without the template, TERT cannot catalyze DNA synthesis, and the TRAP assay would show no extension product. This RNase sensitivity is actually used as a standard negative control in the TRAP assay to confirm that observed activity is genuine telomerase activity.
explanations.C: There are no known inhibitory RNA molecules that suppress TERT catalysis in this manner. RNase treatment destroys TERC, which is essential for activity, not inhibitory. The net effect of RNase is always loss of telomerase function in cell-free assays.
explanations.D: The TRAP assay is performed on cell extracts (lysed cells), so there is no intact nuclear membrane to protect any RNA pool. All cellular RNA, including TERC, is accessible to RNase in the extract. This is why RNase pretreatment serves as an effective negative control—it eliminates all TERC regardless of its original subcellular location.
